data.title: The Biggest Risk For Covered Call ETF Investors And How To Avoid It
data.description:
Introduction

Covered call ETF products serve as a meaningful source of income for many dividend-seeking investors.

The monthly current income streams that usually yield over 10% make these products particularly attractive for investors who want to receive the biggest bang for the buck (i.e., high incremental income impact from each dollar deployed) without involving significant NAV-erosion risks.

Now, the latter part of the statement above is what ultimately gets sacrificed by the key (and I'd say the most frequent) mistake that many covered call ETF investors make.

Based on hundreds of discussions (via my covered call ETF comment sections) with my followers and, importantly, the market data, I can say that more often than not investors have unintentionally assumed too heavy concentration risk into particular market segment - i.e., the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) and/or the S&P 500 (SPY), which are biased towards large-cap growth factor.

Currently, I can see that a heavy price for that is being paid.

In the article below I will try to highlight the problem in an easy-to-understand way and then close the piece with my strategic recommendation on how the situation could be improved.

Problem or the #1 mistake

By investing in covered call ETF products, many investors have not only integrated higher-yielding monthly income sources into their portfolios but also introduced a heavier emphasis on the large-cap growth factor.

As stated above, it is something that I have concluded from my discussions with the followers (those who invest and have shared their covered call ETF picks). But, obviously, just basing my assumption on this observation would be too subjective and/or speculative.

However, the math, or the numbers, doesn't lie. And here they come.

In the table below I've gathered Top 10 largest covered call ETFs out there:

Ticker AuM Exposure
JEPI $43.9B S&P 500
JEPQ $34.5B Nasdaq-100
QQQI $9.4B Nasdaq-100
SPYI $8.2B S&P 500
QYLD $8.1B Nasdaq-100
DIVO $6.7B 24 large-cap names
KNG $3.4B S&P 500 (aristocrats)
GPIX $3.2B S&P 500
GPIQ $3.1B Nasdaq-100
XYLD $3.0B S&P 500

The conclusions are clear.

Almost all of the Top 10 (in terms of AUM) covered call ETF instruments are linked to either SPY or QQQ. The First Trust Vest S&P 500® Dividend Aristocrats Target Income ETF (KNG) and the Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) have a bit of a different angle, but at their core they are still tracking large-cap exposures that stem from the same S&P 500 names.

To illustrate why this might be a problem, I've selected Top 5 names (not all 10 to not overpopulate the chart) and measured their 1-month performance:

The correlations are very strong.

Granted, there are some differences that can come from the applied strategy and what type of options contracts are sold. The Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) is a good example, as it sells at-the-money covered calls, which set a more aggressive upside ceiling but in return provide greater option premium income that tends to work well in falling share price environments.

However, while there indeed are differences, the daylight between ATM (or zero-day expiration) and out-of-the-money covered call ETFs is not that wide.

If the underlying, which in this case is either SPY or QQQ, plunges, the covered call ETFs (irrespective of their option strategy) will follow accordingly. Namely, the covered call ETF products that share the same underlying have rather similar downside risk.

Now, what does it mean? Well, I can see at least the following three consequences:

1. Investors who already have opened pure-play exposures to these (or any other large-cap growth-focused) have magnified the portfolio concentration risk (downside risk) to a particular factor.

2. Investors who have spread their covered call ETF exposures across many different names in order to diversify have potentially failed to do so.

3. Investors have leaned into, arguably, one of the most volatile and risky factors: the large-cap growth factor. In environments like this, when the recessionary risks are looming and the uncertainty levels are elevated, growth tends to be one of the first in line to experience negative repricing. The fact that we entered 2026 with a) 3 strong bull years behind us and b) very high valuations does not help either.

Some might argue here that recently there has been significant covered call ETF product innovation, with many fresh names being sent to the market.

While I agree that this is the case and that in some instances the underlying exposures do indeed differ, the vast majority of the new products are still somehow tied to the same SPY and/or QQQ indices.

For example, not that long ago, YieldMax launched a series of new single-security covered call ETF instruments. Yet, the lion's share of them sit in the Nasdaq-100 index: e.g., YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY), YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY), the YieldMax PLTR Option Income Strategy ETF (PLTY), etc.

Even the largest 0DTE covered call ETFs - the Roundhill Innov-100 0DTE Covered Call Strat ETF (QDTE) and the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE) - are connected to QQQ and SPY, respectively.

And again, if SPY or QQQ falls, then also these products will follow accordingly (the stock-specific covered call ETFs look so bad because their underlyings have experienced significant pullbacks - worse than for the overall indices):

Finally, what I could label as the absolute worst diversification element (if the idea was to diversify the covered call ETF portfolio bucket) is the addition of leveraged covered call ETFs (which sell option contracts against an underlying that is magnified by external leverage).

The NEOS Boosted Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (XQQI) and the NEOS Boosted S&P 500 High Income ETF (XSPI) - both leveraged covered call ETFs against QQQ and SPY, respectively - tell the story very nicely:

Recommended action plan

My suggestion for investors would be to take a fresh look at the covered call ETF investments and see how many of them boil down to the same underlying exposure. My educated guess is that the lion's share of them will be about SPY and/or QQQ indices.

While I fully acknowledge the differences among option contract strategies (e.g., OTM or ATM), I don't think that a diversification on a strategy level is what will move the needle in the context of downside protection (especially if we are talking about large and persistent drawdowns).

In this particular market environment, I wouldn't want to be overexposed to the large-cap growth-focused indices (in my case, it is max 10% to SPY/QQQ-tied names).

Now, I am not saying that all SPY/QQQ-linked covered call ETFs should be divested. In fact, I think that most income-oriented investors have an inherent underemphasis on large-cap growth names because in this area yields tend to be too small to make a difference. So investing in covered call ETFs such as the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) can offer a solution to that without sacrificing the necessary yield component.

However, what I am saying or suggesting is that investors revisit their covered call ETF allocations, contextualize them with the overall portfolio, and if the SPY/QQQ exposure jumps out as too heavy, then consider replacing them with other covered call ETFs that are completely detached from the large-cap growth factor.

There are some, what I call, value-oriented covered call ETFs that might come in handy for both diversification and enhanced downside protection (shield from growth sell-off) purposes. I've elaborated on the value-oriented covered call ETF strategy in my recent piece, Buy Value-Oriented Covered Call ETFs For Relaxed Passive Income.

Finally, let me wrap this article up by providing 5 covered call ETF names to consider further as alternatives to QQQ/SPY-linked peers:

Ticker Exposure AuM Yield My article
MLPI Midstream infrastructure $475M 14.5%* Here
IAUI Gold $389M 12.9%* Here
IYRI Real estate $239M 11.5% Here
IWMI Small-cap value & growth $674M 14.3% Here
NIHI International equity $150M 10.0%* Here

* - Yields are annualized based on the most recent dividend payment (these ETFs don't have a full 12-month operating history).

data.title: AMD: The Catalysts Nobody's Pricing In
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) has doubled in 12 months, and many investors think the easy money has already been made. I disagree, as the stock moved on the momentum of the MI300x and a data center business that went from an afterthought to a potential contender. Today, the biggest catalyst on AMD’s roadmap hasn’t even shipped yet, as it’s set to arrive in the second half of this year. If it lands the way it appears it can, today's share prices are going to look like a bargain in hindsight.

For that reason, and everything outlined in this analysis, my Strong Buy rating on AMD remains in place.

Where does AMD stock stand today?

Before we get into the meat of this, it’s important to see where it currently is, as that helps foresee where it could potentially go from there.

At the time of this publication, AMD stock trades at $220.18 per share. Year to date, the stock has risen just 2.81%. That’s not as strong by semiconductor standards and somewhat undersells the bigger picture, but this performance also accounts for the selloff that started in late 2025, in which almost all tech companies have been affected. Even so, over the past 12 months, AMD stock is still up >163% to shareholders, so the long-term picture still holds up.

That said, AMD stock has dropped almost 10% since the last earnings report, and it’s only now starting to claw back. The selloff was worth paying attention to, given that AMD beat on major metrics in the fourth quarter of 2025. Revenue and earnings per share all came in above what the street had expected, yet, for some reason, the stock still went down.

After a closer look, macroeconomic factors aside, what drove the selloff was potentially management’s own guidance. The next quarter’s revenue (Q1 ’26) is expected to come in at roughly $9.8 billion, which implies a 5% sequential drop from the fourth quarter’s top-line figures. Clearly, for a stock that had doubled through a non-stop acceleration after a long period of decline, even a small hiccup was enough to spook investors.

The market also gave zero credit for the $390 million in surprise China GPU revenue that helped boost the fourth-quarter figures, largely because management would only guide to about $100 million in additional China sales for the next quarter, and investors saw that as a potential hole in the revenue outlook rather than what it most likely was, which was conservative guidance.

But even if we set aside Chinese revenue, the bigger issue was tone. Fourth-quarter earnings came in relatively strong, no questions about it, but the market wanted a bigger promise than management was willing to make. With all the expectations around AMD’s turnaround, anything less gets punished.

Valuation-wise, AMD’s forward price-earnings (GAAP) sits at nearly 50x, which reflects about a ~70% premium relative to the semiconductor sector median. Basically, investors are paying about $50 for every dollar of AMD’s expected earnings over the next 12 months, and compared to the sector median, it certainly looks expensive by traditional standards.

The point is that the skeptical side of the street has been comparing this aspect of AMD against Nvidia (NVDA), which trades at relatively lower ratios than AMD and the sector median. Well, it’s not incorrect- Nvidia is potentially undervalued right now, as noted in my latest Nvidia analysis, considering all the growth prospects in the pipeline.

However, looking solely at this valuation metric isn’t the most optimal approach for companies in the middle of a growth transition like AMD. Again, its potential biggest catalyst arrives in the second half of this year, so if AMD’s earnings jump sharply during that period, which is very likely considering management’s projected numbers and the partnership pipeline, that current valuation would shrink fast heading into 2027.

Also, AMD’s forward PEG ratio (Non-GAAP) already gives us a hint. Right now, it sits at just 0.78x, which also means roughly 41% below the sector median. So the market is potentially pricing in today’s uncertainty while largely overlooking the growth prospects that’s right around the corner, though that likely won’t last long.

What could push AMD higher in 2026 and beyond?

AMD’s single biggest growth driver over the next 12 to 18 months is the launch of its Helios rack-scale platform and the MI450 accelerator, and both of these are expected to ship in the second half of 2026. To be clear, this is not a GPU upgrade where we can expect an upgrade cycle. While that’s good, Helios Rack Scale is AMD’s first fully integrated rack-level system.

I don’t intend to geek out on the specifications because the whole point is, AMD will now be selling an entire computing rack as one product instead of shipping out standalone chips that customers have to piece together on their own, and rack-scale computer systems also come with better margins, and most importantly, this production puts AMD in direct competition with Nvidia’s GB200 NVL72 systems. As someone who at one point spent hours in a past life racking servers in a data center, I can say this is a major upgrade.

But is it enough to overthrow Nvidia? No, not in the least. This new rack-scale system is a step in the right direction toward owning a much larger share of the data center market, and what gives backbone to this ambition is AMD’s partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI), which spans 6 gigawatts of GPU deployment. That alone gives AMD street cred on performance at scale.

Also, the customer list is growing. In its most recent earnings call, management said AMD is in active talks with additional customers on large-scale deployments starting with Helios and MI450. Multiple OEMs have already publicly committed to launching Helios systems in 2026, and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is one of them, building Helios racks with HPE Juniper Ethernet switches.

On that note, the HPE and Juniper aspects deserve more attention than it is getting. That’s because one of Nvidia’s most lethal advantages has always been the tight vertical integration- it has the GPUs, the interconnect that links the GPUs together (NVLink), and all the networking hardware also coming from Nvidia. So, with HPE in the picture, AMD is now building an Nvidia-competing stack without shouldering all the costs alone.

I can see this approach actually holding ground as it mirrors how AMD won the server CPU market over the years. By leaning on the OEM network instead of trying to outbuild the incumbent, and most importantly, OEMs don’t invest engineering resources into building custom systems around a GPU platform unless the customers behind them are asking for it, which in this case, are the hyperscalers. So, the fact that this OEM ecosystem is coming to life means the pull demand for AMD’s rack system is already spoken for.

Now, from a development standpoint, the progress is also worth noting. The MI450 is moving along well, with testing underway at both the chip-level and full rack scale, and management says the timeline remains on track for a second-half launch. According to CEO Lisa Su during the earnings call:

So first of all, I think the MI450 Series development is going extremely well. So we're very happy with the progress that we have. We're right on track for a second half launch and beginning of production. And as it relates to sort of the shape of the ramp and the customer engagements, I would say the customer engagements continue to proceed very well. We have obviously a very strong relationship with OpenAI, and we're planning that ramp starting in the second half of the year going into 2027. That is on track.

And more importantly, a broad group of customers beyond OpenAI is actively working to ramp deployments in the same window, which is also confirmed by Su during the same earnings call. Now, this also means the MI450 cycle will not live and die on a single customer relationship, particularly with OpenAI, as it has the bandwidth to generate diversified revenue growth.

Of course, when it comes to the money, management’s long-term target calls for data center revenue to compound at greater than 60% over the next three to five years.

The cost side of the equation supports this, too. CFO Jean Hu confirmed on the same fourth-quarter earnings call that revenue is expected to grow faster than operating expenses in 2026, which would drive meaningful EPS well above the 2025 figures.

AMD’s non-GAAP opex in 2025 totaled roughly $10.4 billion on $34.6 billion in revenue, or about 30% of revenue. If total revenue climbs to the mid $40 billion range in 2026, representing 30 to 35% growth, while operating expenses grow at something closer to 20% to roughly $12.5 billion, that ratio drops to about 27% of revenue.

Three percentage points of leverage on a $46 billion revenue base is over $1 billion in additional operating income, which, after taxes adds about $0.53 per share- based on ~1.65 billion shares (diluted)- and that’s before counting the earnings contribution from the revenue growth itself. Well, it’s easy to see how the setup could re-rate the stock quickly, but only if the revenue shows up on time.

All that is good, but this is where my deeper conviction sits. If the second half ramp executes on schedule, the acceleration will suddenly be hard to ignore. Remember, data center revenue came in at $5.4 billion in the fourth quarter of 2025, and management is guiding for segment growth that could exceed 60%, which gets us roughly $26.5 billion in data center revenue against the base of $16.6 billion in 2025. And if we buy into Lisa Su’s optimism, we could be looking at as much as $174 billion through FY’30.

Based on the first quarter’s 2026 guidance, the first half looks set to come in soft, which means the back half has to carry most of the incremental revenue to hit the target. Against the full year 2025 revenue of $34.6 billion, that back-half acceleration is what bridges the gap between a quiet first half and the kind of top line that makes the current valuation look reasonable.

To be sure, the market is assigning some probability to this playing out, but it hasn’t fully bought in. A clean execution in the second half would likely trigger a move higher in the stock. That said, no company is immune to risk.

What’s the foreseeable risk around AMD?

One of the most unnoticed risks right now is the gap between how fast operating expenses are growing and how quickly that spending converts into sales. Well, this is not a dealbreaker by any means, but it’s the tension that the market has mostly shrugged off.

This is something that stood out during the fourth-quarter earnings call. Bernstein analyst Stacy Rasgon called out Su on a pattern that has been going on for a while now- every quarter, AMD guides opex higher, only for the actual opex to come in even higher than the guided figure. In the fourth quarter, for example, opex landed at $3 billion, which was up 42% year-over-year. Also, the ZT Systems acquisition layered in extra headcount costs that pushed the bill even higher.

Further into 2026, Jean Hu (AMD’s CFO) was quite upfront that investors should expect continued heavy spending across the MI450 and MI500 chip roadmap, more investment in software to narrow the gap between AMD and its peer, Nvidia, and a much larger sales team. Because of this, the first quarter of 2026 non-GAAP opex is guided to roughly $3.05 billion, or around $12 billion annualized, and this is before the MI450 and Helios revenue ramp has even started.

That’s a heavy bill to carry through the first half, and as you can see, the entire bull case rides on the second half of 2026. Now, if the production ramp slips by even one quarter, which isn’t far-fetched considering the HBM4 supply issues I mentioned in my previous AMD analysis, AMD would be stuck carrying a massive and growing cost base against weak revenue.

And of course, any delayed ramp will all but squeeze margins right when the street is expecting them to expand, and that kind of misalignment could easily lead to an earnings miss in a quarter where the bar is already set high, and if that happens, the market’s reaction wouldn't be pleasing.

What’s my verdict on AMD?

The next two quarters will give us the truth. The second quarter 2026 earnings will be the last report before the MI450 and Helios revenue starts showing up in real volume, and the guidance on that quarter will either lock in the second half narrative or bust the door open for doubt. I expect management to get more specific about customer order visibility as the launch window gets closer.

Until then, the investment thesis comes down to the fact that AMD has never had a product lineup this strong and a market opportunity this wide. The valuation is demanding and uncertain, but on the other side of a clean ramp is a company generating $7 to $8 billion in quarterly data center revenue, with an operating leverage engine that’s about to fire up. With that, and everything outlined above, my Strong Buy rating on AMD stays in place.

data.title: Lantheus Holdings: Tensions From The PDUFA Extension
data.description:
It has been over two years since my last Lantheus Holdings, Inc. (LNTH) article, where I reviewed the market's reaction to Point Biopharma and Lantheus Holdings releasing positive topline results for their radioligand therapy in patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. The SPLASH trial met its primary endpoint, with the progression-free survival (PFS) showing benefits over the control group. However, the market crushed LNTH, but I was still bullish on the ticker's long-term potential. Since then, the ticker is up about 27.5%, but the company publicized that the FDA had decided to do a three-month extension on the PDUFA date for their PET diagnostic kit, OCTEVY (LNTH-2501) (Ga-68 edotreotide). Now, the FDA’s review deadline is delayed to June 29th, 2026, which pushed the share price down to the $75-$76 per share range. Yes, a PDUFA extension is going to push the potential approval and launch of OCTEVY back, so it is not a positive event for the company, but I must stress these events are mostly procedural, with the FDA essentially projecting they need more time to review the company’s submission. According to the company's press release, it looks as if the FDA requires additional time to review the company's manufacturing-related information, and this is not due to any efficacy or safety data. Nonetheless, the FDA has put out a speed bump in the company's efforts to pivot toward high-growth PET radiodiagnostics. Investors need to navigate this time period with a detailed game plan for managing their LNTH position.

I intend to provide some background on OCTEVY and review the company’s press release for the PDUFA extension. In addition, I will provide my views on OTEVY’s extension and how the company requires OCTEVY to be a catalyst to return to growth. Then, I will highlight a couple of risks that investors should consider when managing their LNTH position during this timeframe. Finally, I take a look at the LNTH daily chart to see if I can identify a near-term opportunity to manage my position.

Background on OCTEVY

OCTEVY is a PET-based imaging diagnostic kit that prepares a Ga-68 edotreotide injection for somatostatin receptor-positive neuroendocrine tumors (SSTR+ NETs) that can be used in both adult and pediatric patients. The neuroendocrine tumor market often requires a radiopharmaceutical, and Gallium-68 tracers are well understood, which makes OCTEVY a lower-risk candidate compared to more novel options. So, OCTEVY already has an adoption pathway.

Don’t think that just because there is an established market and familiarity with Ga-68, OCTEVY isn’t cutting edge. PET tracers frequently require centralized preparation outside the treatment settings, but OCTEVY can be prepared on-site. So, the kit-based design should address logistical restrictions and allow for scalable adoption outside specialized treatment centers. Thus, OCTEVY has the potential to take advantage of the established market to accelerate uptake but also possibly expand to new customers and increase penetration.

Reviewing the Press Release

Looking at the company’s press release, the FDA requested extra time to review OCTEVY’s Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls (CMS). Although a PDUFA extension is not common, it does not indicate that the FDA is concerned about OCTEVY’s clinical data. Again, the FDA needs more time for the CMC review, so the agency might be looking at information regarding manufacturing standards, thus helping ensure consistency and safety in the preparation. Keep in mind, OCTEVY is a two-vial kit to be used with Gallium-68 eluate, so the FDA must be confident that all the components can be prepared reliably.

My Thoughts on the Extension

I’m not trying to downplay OCTEVY’s PDUFA extension, but I would also say that I’m not surprised that the FDA requires more time to review the CMC data for a radiopharmaceutical product. OCTEVY involves isotopes like Gallium-68 that must be prepared on-site for PET, so we are talking about highly specialized production and handling methods.

I would be concerned if the FDA required more time to work with Lantheus over the label scope or if there was a late-cycle submission after the agency had questions about subgroup efficacy. One of those requests might indicate that the clinical dataset is showing variability in treatment effect, despite the overall trial posting positive marks. As a result, the FDA might consider moving towards a narrower label that is limited to the clearest responders. So, a company might be looking at a narrowed label, or a post-marketing requirement, or possibly a complete response letter (CRL) if the FDA is uncomfortable moving forward. Again, we don’t have any indication that the FDA is moving in this direction with OCTEVY.

Interestingly, the last time I can remember a radiopharmaceutical needing a PDUFA extension due to CMC information was AZEDRA, which Lantheus attained when it acquired Progenics. AZEDRA was eventually approved. I’m not claiming that OCTEVY will have the same approval process as AZEDRA, but I will stress that there have been radiopharmaceutical products that had a PDUFA extension for CMC and were approved during that extension timeline.

OCTEVY’s Role in the Shift Toward PET Radiodiagnostics

I must concede that OCTEVY does not appear to be a vital cogwheel to Lantheus’ 2026 strategic pivot toward PET radiodiagnostics, which are typically high-growth, high-margin imaging products. However, getting OCTEVY through the FDA and onto the market will establish some clinical momentum to help get their other registration-stage assets across the finish line and continue transitioning their efforts to PET radiodiagnostics.

These programs include the new PSMA PET formulation, PNT2003, and MK-6240, which would also benefit from the existing PET infrastructure for a strong launch.

Certainly, Lantheus needs OCTEVY and its other finish line candidates to get out of the gates quickly. The company has been able to execute its strategy, but the earnings growth did come to an abrupt halt in 2025 as PYLARIFY dealt with competitive pressure, with the company bringing in $1.54B in revenue.

The company did see strong growth from its precision diagnostic products like Neuraceq, but the market is going to require the company to post a strong rebound in 2026 if investors want to see LNTH get back above $100 per share.

Admittedly, OCTEVY isn’t going to get the company back to double-digit revenue growth all by itself in 2026, with one analyst expecting OCTEVY to launch in Q3 with $2M in sales and about $5M for Q4. However, I must point out that OCTEVY is entering the Gallium-68 PET market, which is expected to grow to $3B by 2035, so there is room to grow in the coming years. The company publicized that there are 170K NET patients in the U.S., with each patient getting 1 to 2 scans per year. Use of 50K scans per year at $1.5K per scan could yield $75M in sales in a few years. However, OCTEVY has transitional pass-through reimbursement (TPT), so it could generate over $2K per scan, moving those sales over $100M during the TPT window, which should be through 2029. Perhaps I’m being too conservative, and the volume is much higher than 50K per year, and OCTEVY could be bringing in closer to $150M per year. Regardless, I would like to point out that the company needs every dollar it can get to help offset the PYLARIFY decay and help get this Lantheus back on a growth trajectory, and OCTEVY could help with that.

In fact, the company is expecting Neuraceq and DEFINITY to show some growth and help them pull in between $1.4B and $1.45B in total revenue for 2026. Street analysts agree with management’s outlook for 2026 and expect the company to return to growth for the remainder of the decade.

While it is doubtful that OCTEVY becomes a standalone growth machine, it could be a bridge asset that could contribute to stabilizing the company’s near-term revenue, help accelerate PET adoption, and help reestablish Lantheus’ growth trajectory in the coming years.

Two Risks to Consider

Despite being bullish on Lantheus’ long-term prospects, I must acknowledge a couple of risks that investors should consider when managing their LNTH position while we wait for the FDA’s decision on OCTEVY. First, investors need to consider the concerns about the company’s projected near-term performance. Both the company and Street analysts are expecting the company to take a step back in 2026 for EPS and revenue, so it is possible that any positive updates, including an OCTEVY approval, could be overshadowed by earnings reports showing a year-over-year decrease.

Second, we have to accept that despite my views that this PDUFA extension appears to be minor and procedural, I recognize that additional manufacturing questions or site inspections could push the approval timeline further or even result in a CRL for OCTEVY. Any setback would likely result in a delay in commercialization and revenue.

Despite these risks, I’m maintaining my LNTH conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare Bioreactor Growth Portfolio.

My Plan

I have been lucky to have successfully traded LNTH through a few position cycles over the past seven years. My general strategy is to systematically amass a position at specific trading levels and then look to book enough profits to leave a remnant position to build into a long-term investment.

Presently, LNTH is sitting in a “House Money” position that could be left alone for a long-term investment. However, I’m debating bringing the position out of mothballs under the right conditions. Looking at the daily chart, I don’t see any high-conviction setups that would entice me to pull the trigger in the short term. That being said, the ticker has been sustaining a strong move since November, with most trading days ending in a “Bullish” or “Very Bullish” rating on the Go-No-Go indicator. In addition, the share price is trading above the 200-day EMA and has shown strength on the RSI since December. An anchored VWAP from March is currently applying some downward pressure, but a strong break above could generate enough momentum to break the downward trend line from July 2024. Therefore, I’m going to keep a close eye on LNTH over the next week or two to see if buyers can fuel a strong enough push to alter the ticker’s technical state.

Admittedly, I’m not overly enthusiastic about buying up LNTH. At the same time, we wait for the FDA’s decision, but I have to respect the possibility that the market sees the PDUFA Extension as procedural. The company is going to maximize its current portfolio to potentially offset the decline in PYLARIFY revenue.

data.title: Apple Before Q2: Weaponizing Memory Shortages And The Globalstar Acquisition
data.description:
Introduction

It is wild to think that Apple Inc. (AAPL) has just turned 50 years old. I’ve been an iPhone user for about 12 years now, and all of my gadgets are Apple, including the laptop, the accessories like a mouse, an iPad, a headset, a watch, basically all of the devices I use, except for my PlayStation. But even as an active user, I’ve been pretty skeptical of the stock. Lately, though, the company has been surprising me with how aggressive they are willing to be against their competitors, and this whole story is getting more interesting for me.

There are also rumors that Amazon (AMZN) is eyeing Globalstar (GSAT) (where Apple owns a 20% stake of licensing and satellites and effectively controls the network capacity), which could impact Apple stock. Besides, there is the global memory shortage, and it looks like Apple realized it will be paying more anyway, so it decided to disrupt the competitors on the way. Ahead of the earnings on April 30, the market is clearly paying attention to Apple, with almost 30 positive EPS revisions in the last few months, and while I recognize the effort they are making to capture market share, I’m still not convinced the long-term future looks as bright as the current earnings suggest.

Q1 2026 Earnings

Before I jump into the news, let’s remember what Apple delivered in the last quarter and what to expect from the company right now. First thing I have to say, looking at the numbers, is that it’s insane how much money Apple is making, and the growth the company just presented. I knew the iPhone 17 was a hit because I was one of the people trying to buy and ended up waiting for a few months because of the shortage, but it is completely different seeing it as revenue on the books. They made over $143 billion in a quarter, which is an absolute record. Most of that came from the iPhone 17, which grew 23%. The best part is that the rest of the phone market was flat, and only Apple did so well. Even in China, sales jumped by almost 40%.

Greater China also grew 38% year-over-year, driven by iPhone, which had record upgraders and double-digit growth on switchers.

It’s clear that people are buying these new phones in huge numbers, but it doesn’t mean Apple suddenly started catching the market share. The growth in “switchers,” or people who turned to iPhone after other phones grew double digit, which is definitely great, but doesn’t point to some major breakthrough.

Our installed base reached an all-time high in both Greater China and Mainland China. And we set an all-time record for the upgraders and we saw strong double-digit growth on switchers.

I am not surprised that the vast majority are existing iPhone users who want to get a new phone, because most of their models, which came out in the last 4 years, weren’t great, and iPhone 16 was a straightforward disaster with many bugs, and a very low score on the reviews online. I learned about this, by the way, after my mom got a new iPhone 16 Pro, and the user experience has been terrible. We’ve started researching and realized that she is not the only one who is unhappy with her phone. She, by the way, ended up selling it and getting an iPhone 15 instead. Several other models, like the iPhone 14 Pro, weren’t great, because the glass on the back panel of mine broke under my fingers when I tried to take off the case. This was the reason I switched to the iPhone 17 Pro, which is finally made of aluminum again, and many of my friends did the same for the same reason.

Being in my shoes, you probably see why I’m skeptical. If so many people have switched to this new iPhone, I think the next few models will struggle, and with them, the revenues will be way lower, too.

Looking ahead to the next few months, things might get a little rough. Apple, by the way, claims they are in "supply chase mode," meaning they can't make the iPhone 17 fast enough to keep up with how many people want them, because they’re struggling to get enough of the chips they need. I’m not sure the situation is still the same, and to be honest, together with the fact that Apple just announced its new, cheap MacBook Neo, which operates on the same exact A18 chip, I’m not sure the supply vs. demand really throws this company off as much as the management claims.

And based on that, we're in a supply chase mode to meet the very high levels of customer demand. We are currently constrained. And at this point, it's difficult to predict when supply and demand will balance. The constraints that we have are driven by the availability of the advanced nodes that our SoCs are produced on. And at this time, we're seeing less flexibility in supply chain than normal, partly because of our increased demand that I just spoke about.

On top of that, the memory shortage is going to start impacting the financials very soon. They managed to dodge the high prices last quarter, but they’ve warned investors that the next three months will be more expensive. Still, they expect to keep their profit margins around 48%, which shows how much "extra" they put into the price of their devices.

Another thing the market is very excited about is Apple Intelligence, and analysts often say that Apple had a “patient” approach. As far as I can remember, they were late to the party, didn’t build anything, tried to roll out their “intelligence,” failed miserably twice, and ended up signing with Google (GOOG) for Gemini.

Don’t get me wrong, this might not be the bad thing at all. First, not everyone must spend billions to build the capacity to train the large language models, or LLMs; second, companies like Alphabet should have some benefit as well. At the same time, I don’t get the romanticizing of Apple’s past. If AI spending won’t make the desired returns, we will all claim that Apple was just the smartest of all? I personally won’t, because Apple tried and did not succeed.

These are very different things. They wanted to make Siri intelligent by running the models on the mobile, but it didn’t work out. They tried to build a cloud-based model that could compete with GPT, but it was hallucinating and was very slow. With Google, they realized they were too far behind and decided to accept the situation. Now this current Siri, which is powered by Gemini, is an interesting partnership, because this is a private way to use AI as it runs on their own servers, and not on Alphabet’s, but for now, I am not seeing it as a major moat. I never use these AI features, and I never used Siri before AI. As a matter of fact, I don’t know even one person who would use it on a day-to-day basis. Apple claims they see that the users are using it, but “trying out” could be confused for using as well.

During the quarter, we were excited to see that the majority of users on enabled iPhones are actively leveraging the power of Apple Intelligence.

Memory Shortage

Memory shortage was supposed to be a negative catalyst for this stock, but Apple kind of made me believe they can use it to their benefit, and I love a bloody competition. This could be enough to convince me to buy the stock if the company is really intentionally trying to “kill” the competitors. But let’s start at the beginning.

The global memory shortage we’re seeing right now is driven by the insane demand for AI. Memory giants like Samsung (SSNLF) and Micron (MU) are reallocating their production away from consumer electronics to build high-margin chips for AI data centers, but the demand there is so huge that even that is not enough to cover it. This way, we get a massive shortage for all types of memory, including LPDDR5X, which is the specific type of RAM memory Apple needs. It is used in almost all of Apple’s important devices this year, like the iPhone 17 Pro or the new MacBook Neo. The problem is that without this memory, Apple Intelligence won’t work on the devices, because it requires 12GB of capacity for the iPhone, and 8GB for the laptop. The problem is that Nvidia is buying the same memory for its AI servers, so Apple is in a bidding war.

But here the story gets interesting because Apple decided to be on the offense and not the defense, and it reportedly agreed to pay Samsung a 100% price premium, basically double the market rate (and yes, after the market rate has already gone up), to lock their supply through the middle of the year, beating all of the competitors to it. By doing this, Apple is intentionally taking an extra blow to its margins to "starve out" other producers. It looks like paying for making the situation even worse for the competitors.

Taking into account the new cheap MacBook Neo of $599 for regular customers and $499 for students, they are making a double hit on laptop makers like Asus or Acer, which held this low-cost segment basically forever. To keep it, they already operated on very thin margins, unlike Apple, so this blow to both the memory supply and the cheap segment market share will be very hard for companies to absorb. And even though Apple pays a lot of money for it, they are also winning completely new customers who will end up paying for iCloud, maybe Apple Music, and the rest of the services. It’s a very aggressive way of doing business, and I absolutely love to watch it.

Globalstar Acquisition

I’ve just broken down the rumors about the possible acquisition in a separate article, and what it could mean for Amazon and even Starlink (SPACE). But Apple’s side is also very interesting. I’ve read what the consensus of analysts is for Apple’s outcome if the acquisition goes through, and it is mostly negative. The market believes that if Apple is forced into the deal, the stock may take a hit, but I actually disagree here.

I think that for Apple shareholders, the potential deal is a "win-win" scenario that most of the market hasn’t recognized yet. If the acquisition moves forward, Apple has incredible leverage. They hold a $1.5 billion investment, which gives them a 20% stake in a subsidiary, which means they have 20% on all the licenses and satellites, and it grants Apple 85% of the network capacity. It means that Amazon would effectively be paying to become Apple’s primary infrastructure provider. From a shareholder perspective, this is brilliant, because Apple would keep its exclusive satellite features for the iPhone (they decide where the 85% of the network capacity goes), but all of the regulatory headaches of maintaining a satellite constellation is passed to its biggest competitor, Amazon, from Globalstar.

Beyond the operational perks, there is a significant financial upside. While their 20% stake is in the satellite-owning subsidiary and not the public stock (the SA reader caught this legal distinction), a $9 billion valuation for the parent company would likely trigger a massive revaluation of Apple's interest. If Amazon wants to take total control over both the mother firm and the subsidiary, it would have to negotiate a buyout of Apple's 20% stake, and I can only imagine how much money Apple would want for that. Anyway, there is still no confirmation that the acquisition is even being discussed.

Valuation and Conclusion

Despite Apple’s aggressive strategy and the great fundamentals in the last quarter, looking at the current valuation, it is clear that even in the middle of a broader market sell-off, Apple is the most expensive company in the Mag 7, not including Tesla (TSLA). With a Non-GAAP Forward P/E of over 30, the stock is trading at nearly a 40% premium to the sector median. Even more concerning is the PEG Non-GAAP (Forward) ratio of 2.92, which is a staggering 121% higher than the sector average. This suggests that investors are paying a massive premium for growth that may not actually materialize at the rate the current price implies.

I admire their ability to play aggressively against competitors by overpaying for RAM. This is a bold move, and being on the offense usually means having more control over the situation. But still, their revenue is mostly iPhone-made, and as I explained in this article, I don’t believe there is a chance for Apple to make the same results two years in a row. Between the looming expiration of cheap memory contracts and a valuation that is already stretched, I am rating Apple as Hold again, and not buying the stock myself, although I’ll most likely continue being a loyal customer.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pearson: A Stock To Watch As Margins Expand
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Pearson plc (NYSE:PSO) is trading at a forward P/E of 14.59x in line with the sector median of 14.60x. However, based on my analysis, the stock deserves to trade at a premium rather than in line with or below the sector. The company has expanded operating margins from 9% to 17% over five years, increased free cash flow at 18% annually, improved Return on Capital by 470 basis points to 11.3% and delivered consistent operating leverage. In my view, the ongoing transition toward AI integration will be the key catalyst for the stock.

Pearson is a global education and learning company headquartered in London with operations across the world. It operates through five segments: Assessment and Qualifications, Virtual Learning, Higher Education, English Language Learning, and Enterprise Learning and Skills. The company is listed on the NYSE as ADR.

Competitive Position In Education Technology Market

The company competes in the education industry against companies including McGraw-Hill Education (MH), Chegg (CHGG), Coursera (COUR), and various EdTech startups across the world. The Assessment and Qualifications is the largest segment at approximately 45% of total revenue for Pearson. In this market, it competes with Prometric. For the Enterprise Skilling segment, it competes with LinkedIn Learning and Coursera for Business.

According to Grand View Research, the global education technology market is projected to reach $437 billion by 2033 from $187 billion in FY 2025, growing at a CAGR of 10%. Pearson's revenue increased by 4% in FY 2025, which is almost half of the industry's projected growth rate. To capture higher market share, management has identified three structural megatrends driving demand including upskilling demand, demographics, and AI. The company has been transitioning towards AI adoption and the partnerships it has secured with Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Google Cloud confirmed this pivot. In my view, the AI adoption thesis is what most investors are underweighting and still see this company as a declining textbook publisher.

Management confirmed this direction explicitly in the FY 2025 results: "Continued to lead with the application of innovative technologies, deepening and scaling AI across our offering, driving measurable improvements in learner outcomes and saving educators meaningful time, whilst embedding AI as a foundational capability within Pearson."

It may happen that generative AI tools compete with Pearson's content and assessment products and students start using AI for test preparation. There is certainly an AI risk associated with the company. However, in my view Pearson's most defensible competitive position is built on institutional relationships and proprietary data. In fact, the company has already refocused its entire product strategy toward embedding AI into its own products. In nutshells, the company has turned a potential threat into a structural advantage.

Operating Leverage And AI Efficiency

It is the operating margin expansion thesis that is important for investors to understand in this company. In FY 2025, underlying sales increased by 4% compared to adjusted operating profit growth of 6%. This gap between revenue growth and operating profit growth is an important indicator. When operating profit increases faster than revenue, it signifies operating leverage. This is further confirmed by the margin expanding from 16.9% in FY 2024 to 17.2% in FY 2025. But to understand the scale of this improvement, it is worth going through the long-term trend from the chart above.

The adjusted operating profit has almost doubled from £313 million in FY 2020 to £614 million in FY 2025. The margin has expanded from 9% in FY 2020 to 17% in FY 2025. In my view, this consistent 800 basis point margin expansion is worth noting achieved during currency headwinds, economic headwinds, or operational disruption during COVID.

For investors, the important question is what is driving this momentum. In my view, there are two reasons for this margin improvement in the company. First, the company has pivoted toward higher-margin digital products compared to lower-margin physical content. Second, AI-driven cost savings are increasing in the operational systems.

The company has provided specific data on cost efficiency achieved through AI adoption across its operational systems.

  • 40%+ reduction in content editing time,
  • ~30% reduction in translation costs,
  • ~25% lower content alignment costs,
  • ~40% reduction in customer service volumes,
  • More than 130,000 customer interactions are now handled by AI voice agents.

In my view, the cost structure will continue to improve as AI is integrated further into operational systems. For FY 2026, management has guided further margin expansion and has estimated average margin growth of 40 basis points per year for the medium term. Based on this analysis, we can conclude that the margin expansion story will emerge further, and it is a must-track indicator for investors in the next earnings report.

Cash Flow And Balance Sheet: 4 Key Insights Must For Investors

The table above shows four key insights that investors should go through to understand the operational efficiency and net leverage of the business.

  • The first insight is that free cash flow has been a positive trend from FY 2021. FCF has grown from £229 million in FY 2020 to £527 million in FY 2025 at an 18% CAGR. This is three times faster than revenue growth of 5% CAGR over the same period, which shows operational efficiency with better cost control. It is worth noting that it is a multi-year uptrend that reflects a business that is fundamentally strong.
  • The second insight is Return on Capital. ROC has improved from 6.6% in FY 2020 to 11.3% in FY 2025, a 470 basis point improvement. But here is important metric for investors. Pearson's weighted average cost of capital is approximately 7.8% compared with ROC of 11.3%. In theory, when a business earns returns above its WACC, it creates value for the shareholders.
  • Next insight is operating cash conversion consistency. Management calculates this as free cash flow divided by adjusted earnings. The average cash conversion across the entire period is 100%, which indicates the company converts almost all of its adjusted operating profit into actual operating cash flow. This is an important quality signal for investors.
  • Last insight is the net debt position and what it means for investors. Net debt has increased from £463 million in FY 2020 to £1,069 million in FY 2025. This may look concerning at first glance, but that is not the case here. The net leverage ratio stands at 1.3x within management's self-imposed cap of 2.0x. This means the company has leverage headroom to increase debt for acquisitions or strategic investments without needing to raise equity. This indicates balance sheet flexibility and not financial trouble.

Now, how the company allocates its surplus cash to shareholders is equally important for investors to understand.

The dividend per share has grown every year from 19.5 pence in FY 2020 to 25.2 pence in FY 2025 at a CAGR of 5%. Doesn't this consistency matter? This indicates the confidence of management in stable and predictable cash flows. In total, it has returned £1.4 billion to shareholders since FY 2020 through dividends and buybacks. A further £350 million buyback is planned for FY 2026.

In a nutshell, it is not just generating strong free cash flow but also returning that cash to shareholders through improving dividends and buybacks. In my view, this capital return performance deserves as much attention from investors as other indicators.

Enterprise Skilling and Early Careers Emerging as Key Growth Drivers

Most investors tracking Pearson focus on their largest Assessment and Qualifications segment and its near-term growth rates. But higher returns can be achieved by focusing on the next phase of growth for the company. The management has identified three structural megatrends which include upskilling demand, demographics, and AI. In my view, to support this trend they have identified Enterprise Skilling and Early Careers as medium-term growth drivers.

The Enterprise Learning and Skills segment grew 6% in FY 2025 with operating margin expanding from 7% to 10%. For investors, the forward-looking comments are most important for evaluating a stock. In the above slide from the FY 2025 earnings presentation, you can see "hundreds of millions in incremental revenue up to 2030." This indicated that within this segment, multi-year revenue contracts are already signed with technology partners. The company has signed a multiyear partnership agreement with Microsoft to co-develop AI-powered learning platforms and assessments. Similar partnership agreements are signed with AWS, Google Cloud and other technology companies. In total, there are 49 Advanced and Elite customers using the Enterprise Learning and Skills product offerings. According to management, the Early Careers segment has an estimated addressable market of $6 billion. The segment focuses on career readiness platforms, technical education curriculum and licensing exams. The company is growing this offering through eDynamic Learning and a virtual school network.

In my view, both initiatives are already positioned to capture the growth in the market and strong momentum can bring upside opportunity for the stock.

Valuation: A Quality Business Trading At A Reasonable Price

Based on the valuation data from Seeking Alpha, Pearson is trading at a reasonable valuation compared to the sector median but at a meaningful discount to its own five-year historical average.

On a forward non-GAAP P/E basis, it trades at 14.59x compared to the sector median of 14.60x. However, when we compare it to a 5-year average of 18.27x, the stock is trading at a 20% discount to its historical valuation. I believe a business that has consistently improved over the last 5 years with higher margins, strong FCF, and improving ROC should not be trading at a 20% discount to its own historical multiple.

However, investors have to closely watch the consistency in margin expansion, integration of AI in operational systems, and revenue trends. As an issue in margin expansion or AI integration could be a negative catalyst for the stock.

Investment Risk

Currency Headwinds - Given its global operations, this is a persistent risk. Therefore, a stronger pound can negatively impact earnings.

AI Disruption Risk - Generative AI tools could be a major challenge for the company. If students begin relying on these tools instead of Pearson’s products, it may reduce demand for paid content subscriptions. The company has already started integrating AI into its offerings, but the risk of free AI tools cannot be ignored.

CFO Transition Risk - Sally Johnson is stepping down after nearly 26 years at Pearson, and Simon Robson joined in March 2026. While the transition is structured, any leadership change carries execution risk.

Final Thoughts

Pearson is trading at a reasonable valuation compared to the sector median but at a meaningful discount to the 5-year historical average. In my view, Pearson is poised to outperform driven by consistent margin expansion, strong free cash flow generation, AI-powered cost efficiency, and the emerging Enterprise Skilling and Early Careers growth drivers.

data.title: Worthington Enterprises: Investing To Simplify And Grow
data.description:
Shares of Worthington Enterprises, Inc. (WOR) have been consolidating a bit as of late. Shares have come in a bit, all while the company sees solid and accelerating organic growth, with more M&A efforts set to drive further growth of the business.

I like these moves, with appeal increasing rapidly in my book, making me upbeat here. Shares are trading at mid-teen earnings multiples, while leverage is modest, organic growth is reported, and the business is becoming simpler. This makes me turn more upbeat here, willing to get involved again on dips here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

About Recent Events

In mid-January, Worthington closed on the acquisition of LSI Group, a deal originally announced in December. At the time, Worthington announced a $205 million purchase of metal roof component manufacturer LSI Group.

The purchase includes leading brands like BPD, Logan Stampings, LSI Metal Fabrication, and Roof Huggers, with the business producing standing seam metal roof clips and retrofit components for the commercial metal roof market.

Founded in 1968, the company has two locations in Logansport, providing such equipment, which is needed for many commercial roofs, typically only having 20-year commercial lifespans. More details were announced at the second quarter conference call, when management indicated that the company “only” generates $51 million in sales, yet over $22 million in adjusted EBITDA, indicating that margins are very high and that acquisition multiples look fair.

Pro Forma Implications

Towards the end of March, Worthington reported its third-quarter results, that is, for the quarter that ended in February. Third-quarter sales rose by nearly a quarter to $378 million, driven by a 35% increase in building product sales to $224 million, making up 60% of above-average margins. This is complemented by a slower-growing and slightly lower-margin consumer product segment.

GAAP operating profits of $31 million and change resulted in margins equal to 8% of change, up from near 7% levels in the third quarter of 2024. That only tells part of the story in the case of Worthington, which has many joint ventures and unconsolidated affiliates. These include ceiling solutions with companies like WAVE and Armstrong and metal framing like ClarkDietrich, the latter of which has been struggling a bit.

Its equity interest in such ventures contributed about $30 million and change to quarterly results, actually down a bit from the year before. Combined with its operations, net earnings are up 15% to $45 million and change, with adjusted earnings up thirteen cents to $0.92 per share. At this pace, the company is on track to earn well over $3 per share for the year here.

Net debt is reported at $308 million, which is no issue at all with the consolidated ventures posting EBITDA close to the same number. Net debt has risen following the purchase of LSI and Elgen earlier this year, with the combined purchase price coming in at $305 million.

With nearly 50 million shares trading at $52 per share, the company commands a $2.6 billion equity valuation and a $2.9 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 2 times reported sales, but that, of course, excludes the implied sales contribution from the unconsolidated ventures.

What Now?

The reality is that the underlying results of Worthington have been strong, with third-quarter organic sales up 14%, notably driven by water and (data center) cooling construction, with M&A efforts adding nicely to that.

All this is rather compelling. With earnings seen well over $3 per share this year, despite some cyclicality in the business, I am getting more upbeat here. As shares have fallen from levels in the mid-sixties last summer, when I called shares fully valued, to $52 per share, the earnings multiple has come down to about 16-17 times earnings. I liked the combination of falling share prices with some real operating momentum, providing support to the valuation from both sides.

This is a reasonable earnings multiple, given the promising joint ventures, reasonable earnings multiples, modest leverage, and full contribution seen from recent dealmaking going forward. This should result in real earnings growth, provided that the situation around Iran does not get completely out of hand. Direct peer comparisons are difficult, given the large non-consolidated equity share from joint ventures. However, valuations look rather compelling given the historical standards of the company itself.

The picture around Worthington has become much clearer, even as there is a large joint venture part of the business. Worthington has simplified business a lot in recent times, having divested the lower-margin and cyclical Worthington Steel, Inc. (WS) business, while it cashed in on a profitable stake in Nikola and divested its sluggish Sustainable Energy Solutions segment.

A Final Take

The truth is that I am a bit more upbeat here on Worthington, with shares down over ten dollars since the summer, while the third quarter has been strong, and continued M&A has been embarked upon.

Given all these developments, I am turning more upbeat here. With earnings power seen firmly above $3 per share, Worthington continues to become a simpler business. Moreover, the company seems consistent and has shown growth, driven by exposure to data centers, among others, while the company is furthermore increasing its shares of fully owned businesses in relation to joint ventures. This is encouraging, as the business seems to have become less cyclical over time, a trend continuing today, which makes me more upbeat here.

Given all this, I am gradually turning more upbeat about the shares here. The combination of lower expectations, solid organic growth performance, some M&A, and secular growth seen here makes me more upbeat. While there are many developments taking place left and right, reported headline numbers underestimate the real sense of progress made.

data.title: STRC: The Digital Credit Opportunity
data.description:
I've been watching the rise of "digital credit" as a topic. The recent weakness in private credit is one big reason investors are paying more attention. Naturally, they're asking, "Where else can I get yield?" Because of that, I wanted to approach the topic myself. I'll use Stretch (STRC), a preferred equity issue from Michael Saylor's Strategy (MSTR), kind of like a case study. Then I'll give my recommendation.

Explaining what digital credit is

I quibble, but "digital credit" is a bit of a misnomer. After all, I said I'm going to talk about a preferred stock, which isn't credit. Credit refers to debt. Maybe one day people will call it "digital income," but for now this is the term.

We might also ask what makes it digital. Credit cards have always been digital, but they aren't counted here. The "digital" part refers to crypto treasury assets, like the Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holdings of MSTR. Saylor spelled it out in his keynote address at Strategy World 2026.

You know, what is digital credit? Well, digital credit is built on digital capital. Well, digital capital is Bitcoin.

Saylor also defined "digital capital" as economic wealth stored digitally, which abstracts his concept to other cryptocurrencies, not just BTC. Perhaps it will apply to tokenized assets as well. The connection to digital capital is the key. Any fixed-income security that a digital asset treasury ("DAT") like MSTR uses to raise capital could be considered digital credit.

So it's kind of like crypto-backed fixed income.

Source of cash flow

Most fixed income is backed by some kind of cash flow, though. Private credit often gets paid back by profits of the borrowers. Strategy depends on the volatility of BTC in order to pay dividends and other costs of capital.

The price history of MSTR shows that it's more volatile than BTC. Strategy uses this as a feature. Dave Birnbaum of Forbes wrote about the theory of Strategy's capital allocation:

First, that in a five year time frame, bitcoin will appreciate – about 25% per year on average. Second, that MicroStrategy will continue to be able to borrow money at lower annualized interest rates than the annualized appreciation of bitcoin. This combination of assumptions, which have held true so far, allow MicroStrategy to “sell the volatility” of bitcoin’s short-term price to options traders.

Strategy doesn't need to liquidate BTC on the upside; they can just offer more MSTR at a premium to holdings. This gives them cash to pay costs of capital and to acquire more BTC.

This may not be easy over time. MSTR reported an unrealized loss of $14.5B in Q1 this year because BTC is down. The same release noted that they bought $330M of BTC from 4/1 to 4/6. About two-thirds was from money raised through STRC offerings. It confirms that STRC is the flagship digital credit offering of Strategy. Saylor originally said this in the Q4 2025 earnings:

STRC is going to continue to season, continue to harden, continue to stabilize...This is the flagship product of the company, right? At this point, everything we're doing in the capital structure is to improve the liquidity, decrease the volatility, increase the AUM, increase the creditworthiness, decrease the risk and improve the standing of Stretch, right?

MSTR's price weakness is also why they relied on it less than STRC recently. This reduced dilution, but it also grew their dividend obligations. Over time this is a balancing act for Strategy. It may be a tough one.

Variable, monthly dividend feature

STRC gives Strategy wiggle room because it pays a monthly, variable dividend. Right now, it works out to an 11.5% yield, but that can change. The prospectus for STRC explains they can adjust the monthly payment with their "sole and absolute discretion." But it does limit itself to reductions of 25 basis points, plus any excess change in SOFR rates in the period.

The plan is to adjust the dividend so that STRC regularly trades at $100 per share and make it a non-volatile asset. If STRC appreciates, they'll cut the dividend each month until it returns to $100. If it goes down, they'll hike it until it recovers.

I think it's a strange feature, but then digital credit is strange territory. It gives us an idea of what other DAT issuers might do down the road. Control over the cost of capital is a key feature of digital credit. Lack of a maturity date is another. The prospectus even says they can redeem the whole issue whenever they want.

The price returns above show that STRC has been very stable, much like a fixed-income product, even while BTC has been down. So far, it looks like Strategy has formed a rare security that combines high yield with price stability.

This was accomplished by hiking the dividend. Monthly payments rose from $0.80 to $0.96. I think cuts could be down the road, especially if BTC & MSTR recover and create more confidence in STRC.

Fixed income, mixed feelings

There are some things to like about STRC. You don't often find stable investments with double-digit yields. Strategy's dashboard shows some favorable data points too. Current holdings support over 47 years of dividend payments. Total preferred capitalization makes up $10.3B of a $53.3B BTC portfolio. That looks like they could repay the balance, to me. Monthly payments are treated as return of capital, not income or qualified dividends, so there can be tax benefits here.

I see potential problems too. We could say they come down to underwriting, as with private credit. Digital credit has the underwriting risk of BTC. Now, BTC has no organic yield. That's why Strategy made income securities. It's a cryptocurrency validated by its proof-of-work construct. I still don't know how it will ultimately be used in the real world. BTC has a supply cap to protect it from inflation, but it's no more "real" than fiat.

What does digital credit do if BTC doesn't appreciate? Well, STRC might have to cut its dividends. An 11.5% yield could fall dramatically, to something like 4%. Strategy might have to redeem the whole issue and close shop if BTC doesn't go anywhere for a few years. This layer of principal protection is a big advantage of STRC over MSTR. The downside is that investors would need to find yield elsewhere at that point.

Because it at least has this, I'm okay giving a Hold rating to STRC. The income is too uncertain to warrant a Buy rating at this moment, though.

Takeaways on digital credit

What can we learn about digital credit? Well, Strategy and STRC show how it's a loose idea of credit and one that will probably keep evolving. STRC investors can get high yields with stable value for now. They also don't have the same protections as true credit investors. I expect other DATs will develop their own preferreds as "digital credit," and I expect them to be creative on specifics too.

Other DATs may hold different cryptocurrencies. Some of them have organic yield. Differences like that can change the risk factors for each issuer. It may also affect how they structure their own preferreds.

So STRC is an interesting case study. It shows that Strategy carefully tailored this security to suit their own goals based on BTC. Other issuers could mimic this, but we can also expect just as much creativity ahead.

data.title: Fault Lines And Flashpoints: Navigating Credit Markets Through A Geopolitical Shock
data.description:
By Matt Freund, CFA, Christian Brobst, and Chuck Carmody, CFA

So much has happened in the past month that investors may have forgotten how constructive the markets were at the beginning of the year. 2026 began as a continuation of 2025’s positive credit environment. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act provided fiscal tailwinds to the economy, while the labor market held unemployment steady despite immigration-driven supply constraints. At the same time, the Federal Reserve cut rates in December, and markets were expecting two or three additional cuts during the year. Ten-year Treasury yields were drifting lower (bond prices were increasing), and credit spreads were near cycle tights. The technical backdrop remained supportive. Then on February 28, the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran, and the calculus changed materially.

Unsurprisingly, the most direct impact has been on energy markets (oil and natural gas). Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global fossil fuels flow, removed critical supply from the market. Brent crude, which traded near $60 per barrel at the start of the quarter, surged well above $100 in March. Gasoline prices in the US rose sharply as a result. The secondary impacts on other markets quickly followed as fertilizer, helium, and carbon-dependent markets soared in price. While the US remains in a strong supply position, shortages and rationing are spreading to Europe and emerging markets. It remains to be seen how these energy-constrained economies adjust, which lower-value exports will be curtailed, and what the disruption will mean for US supply chains.

The Treasury market no longer expects the Fed to cut rates this year. Yields rose across the curve as bonds sold off. Fortunately, corporate credit entered the conflict on solid footing. Defaults (including distressed exchanges) through February remained well below long-term averages at 2.1%. High-yield leverage held under 4x, modestly above the 25-year average but not alarming. Interest coverage had stabilized. Strong inflows and robust new-issue demand had kept spreads compressed through February. High-yield spreads have widened from +266 basis points entering the quarter to slightly more than +300 as of late March, with CCC-rated credits underperforming after widening roughly +100 basis points during the quarter. Investment-grade spreads have also moved wider, reaching +90 at month’s end, leaving the market only 15 basis points wide of cycle tights.

Looking Ahead

We believe the constructive environment that existed prior to the conflict is likely to reassert itself. The fiscal tailwinds from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act remain intact. The labor market, while softening, has not broken, and US energy production insulates the US economy from shortages and minimizes the overall impact of price hikes.

Our base case is that the conflict, while severe, resolves or stabilizes within a couple of months, allowing Hormuz traffic to resume. Under this scenario, energy prices retreat by mid-year, the inflationary pulse proves transitory, and the Fed resumes easing in the back half of 2026. This would turn out to be similar to the 1990–1991 Gulf War. Credit spreads widened sharply but recovered quickly once the conflict was resolved—painful, disruptive, but ultimately finite.

The risk case involves prolonged closure of the strait, continued attacks on energy infrastructure, and oil sustained above $150 per barrel. This raises the risk of stagflation in the US and could force the Fed to stay on the sidelines or even introduce the possibility that the next move is a hike (though we continue to believe hikes are unlikely). History has shown that every major oil shock of the past five decades has been followed by an economic slowdown; the question is whether this oil shock lasts weeks or months.

Positioning Implications

Our defensive credit positioning, established over multiple prior quarters as credit spreads compressed to historically tight levels, has proven well-timed. The migration to higher credit quality across strategies, accomplished while largely preserving income characteristics, left our portfolios better insulated from the spread widening at the lower end of the ratings spectrum. As credit spreads widen (bond prices fall), we expect more attractive opportunities to deploy capital. For now, we maintain our quality bias as developments unfold.

While near-term inflation pressure argues against Fed cuts, large fiscal deficits and term premium concerns argue against a secular decline in long rates. Despite the recent shocks, Calamos Short-Term Bond Fund (CSTIX) remains positioned longer than benchmark duration, while Calamos Total Return Bond Fund's (CTRIX) duration remains short of benchmark, reflecting continued caution on long-end rates. For Calamos High Income Opportunities Fund (CIHYX), where interest rate sensitivity is a lower-order driver of returns, the fund is positioned short of benchmark duration.

Within credit, we are monitoring energy sector exposures carefully and emphasizing domestic producers over energy-intensive industrial issuers (chemicals, transportation, manufacturing) that could face rising input costs and margin pressures until the conflict is resolved. Spread widening has begun to improve relative value, but the current environment of elevated uncertainty leaves us selectively adding to credit exposure where we are being adequately compensated for the elevated risks.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Cycle Persists Through Geopolitical Disruption
data.description:
By John Hillenbrand, CPA

The US economy entered the year in a mid-cycle expansion supported by fiscal tailwinds, easing monetary policy, and a capital-spending boom driven by artificial intelligence. The war in Iran and its effects on energy markets have introduced competing forces: higher commodity prices squeezing real incomes, tighter financial conditions, and renewed uncertainty over supply chains. The central question for investors is whether this shock is large enough to end the expansion or is a disruption that the cycle can absorb. We believe it is the latter. Growth may slow but not contract, the labor market is softening but not breaking, headline inflation is rising but core remains contained, and capital spending continues.

Our January outlook argued that above-consensus US growth would persist as the tariff drag faded, fiscal support from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) flowed through, and the Fed eased toward short-term rates of 3.00% to 3.25%. Economic data through February confirmed that view. The war in Iran disrupted the trajectory in March, with a rise of more than 50% in the price of oil, a 60-basis point tightening in financial conditions, and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The fund (CGIIX) returned –3.8% for the quarter, ahead of the S&P 500 Index’s –4.3% decline. Selection within equities contributed positively, although the portfolio’s elevated equity sensitivity, consistent with our mid-cycle posture, was a headwind in a down market.

Growth Drivers Remain Intact

The positive forces supporting economic expansion have not been impaired. Lagged effects of last year’s rate cuts are still flowing through, fiscal support from the OBBBA is additive to consumer and corporate spending, defense and energy security infrastructure investment is accelerating, and the AI infrastructure buildout has become a meaningful driver of the real economy, contributing roughly a third of real-GDP growth last year. The hyperscalers have shown no inclination to pull back; their capital spending guidance has ratcheted higher through each successive reporting season, and their suppliers are converting that spending into earnings at exceptional incremental margins.

The AI investment thesis is evolving in ways that matter for positioning. Coding assistance has achieved strong adoption but has created volatility in application software names as investors debate disruption risk versus embedded AI benefits; we are waiting for earnings to provide more data points before adding there.

More important for the cycle, agentic AI adoption outside of coding is emerging as a growth driver; industries with the highest exposure to LLM-automatable tasks are showing the highest rates of business AI deployment, suggesting companies are finding productive applications for real-world labor tasks. Early evidence suggests companies deploying these models at scale are converting adoption into revenue at rates that support the spending trajectory. We are monitoring for the inflection point where hyperscaler cloud and AI services’ profit growth outpace capital spending growth, which would validate the return-on-investment case.

Risks and the Inflation Question

The forces opposing the expansion are real but, in our assessment, manageable. Higher energy prices are squeezing real incomes, with the burden falling disproportionately on the bottom 60% of the income distribution, where the combined effects of tariffs, benefit adjustments, and the oil shock amount to roughly a full percentage point drag on disposable income. Central banks may delay rate cuts as they assess the persistence of the energy price shock. Private debt default rates running at several multiples of commercial bank loan book defaults are a source of stress if capital markets tighten. The labor market is softening but not breaking, with slowing demand balancing reduced supply from immigration restrictions. That balance is fragile.
