The critical distinction in our framework is between a headline inflation shock that remains confined to energy and one that broadens into wages and inflation expectations. We see more parallels with 1990–91 and 2000, where energy shocks occurred against moderate demand and roughly neutral policy, than with 2021–22, when broad supply disruptions coincided with expansionary policy and tight labor markets. The oil intensity of US GDP has declined substantially, domestic shale provides a partial offset, and core disinflation from the waning tariff impact should outweigh the energy price effect over the coming months. This assessment supports our decision to maintain mid-cycle equity sensitivity rather than shift to a defensive posture.

Positioning and Outlook

Our positioning reflects this framework. We concentrated the fund's portfolio in areas where we see real growth tailwinds and improving returns on capital: AI infrastructure and semiconductor equipment, targeted pharma and biotech, energy innovation and security, and international markets where valuations offer favorable entry points. The war in Iran has reinforced the case for domestic energy infrastructure, a theme we had been building before the disruption.

We rotated healthcare to active stock selection where we see improving returns on capital; trimmed rate-sensitive financials as near-term easing expectations shifted; retained core bank holdings where the yield curve thesis remains intact; and took profits on semiconductor convertible winners where the risk-reward had narrowed. We are using convertible securities and options to adjust the portfolio’s risk-reward profile in this environment.

We assign the highest probability to a base case in which the Strait of Hormuz returns to normal operations toward late April, energy prices ease in the second half, and the economic damage remains contained. The upside case sees rapid conflict resolution and accelerating AI monetization; the downside sees the war persisting through mid-year with inflation broadening into core measures.

The catalysts that will determine which path materializes are the trajectory of the war, the inflation data over the next two months, and first-quarter hyperscaler earnings guidance. A favorable resolution would validate a return to the more constructive posture we held entering the year. An extended conflict that pushed inflation into wages and core prices, a decisive break in labor market data, or a downward revision to hyperscaler spending guidance would prompt us to de-risk through the convertible and options sleeves of the portfolio. The mid-cycle thesis remains intact, and we are positioned to participate as clarity returns.

Original Post

data.title: Palantir: Hard To Replace, Harder To Ignore At The Core Of Business AI
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Artificial Intelligence is scaling in layers and the AI stalwarts are all playing an important role.

For example, NVIDIA (NVDA) increases the computing power. Furthermore, companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) convert the power into enterprise platforms. Then there is OpenAI which functions to keep lifting model capability.

Enterprise deployment is the problem in this chain. Most companies are unable to transform AI into something that can be implemented in everyday business. The majority of AI projects fail to pass testing due to messy data, increased costs or risk controls.

Palantir (PLTR) is the solution to this issue, to say the least.

Its Artificial Intelligence Platform links decisions, workflow and results in a seamless manner and becomes core to daily operations. The more it is used by the teams, the more difficult it is to substitute it.

This dependency is already reflected in the numbers. The momentum continued through Q4 FY 25 as Palantir closed some big deals and current clients increased usage along with budgets. With time, growth goes further into workflows and makes the platform a fundamental aspect of how businesses are run.

The momentum is also transferred into the stock price. PLTR trades at around $148 (as of writing), and has support at around $143-$145. This arrangement is in line with the business trend and short-term signals show that it has more upside.

Basically, the platform is so ingrained in everyday business that the risk is mitigated even when growth decelerates slightly. The Maven program-of-record is also a hot catalyst and it would also provide stability to the company.

Overall, I assign PLTR a Strong Buy at this point.

Palantir Sits at the Control Layer

Palantir’s Ontology lies at the heart of its system and bridges decisions in the enterprise to the real world.

After that, it transforms those decisions into workflows with data writeback. That puts it between data, models and execution and forms a layer that is difficult to replace. The process of integration increases with time and dependency begins to form.

Nevertheless, in zooming out, Enterprise AI governance is the bigger prospect.

Most AI projects fail post-testing as discussed earlier and companies are also not able to see clear results in many situations.

Palantir allows companies to work with various models, with some level of control. Thus, when orchestration is the primary point of bottleneck, the company controlling that layer captures the value.

Interestingly, Ryan Taylor, Palantir’s Chief Revenue Officer, claimed in the Q4 FY 25 earnings call,

“Our customers aren't tentatively trying AI; they're committing to it at scale with Palantir Technologies Inc. as the driving force.”

This is supported by the figures. The company has ended up closing 180 deals above $1 million and 61 deals above $10 million in Q4. Well, this suggests a huge enterprise demand.

The total contract value was up to $4.26 billion in terms of the pipeline of committed business.

Net dollar retention came in at 139%, which means existing customers are expanding their usage. Moreover, revenue per top 20 customer jumped 45% YOY to $94 million. These statistics reflect actual adoption on the team and budget levels.

Expansion is not only about the number of new customers. Palantir closed Q4 with 954 customers, a 34% upswing. In the meantime, U.S. commercial revenue bumped 137% YOY.

There is a larger narrative to this. The platform is spreading inside existing accounts. There is an upswing in the number of teams joining the system, an increase in the volume of work traveling through it as well as budget settings.

Honestly speaking, the figures are encouraging and such performance will bring about a sustained competitive edge that will continue to spur growth.

AIP Creates Sticky Organizational Dependency

Palantir creates a moat by getting deeply involved with the companies.

The AIP is confused with a simple LLM interface by most. Nevertheless, the platform is a workflow engine that bridges AI and operational data.

This point of difference is crucial since Palantir is more focused on security and governance measures such as granular permissions and audit trails. All these properties make AIP more of a policy layer, instead of a simple chat box.

With the enormous responsibility claims that come with production AI, this is exactly the kind of guardrails that it is seeking.

The budget is directed towards tools that are trustworthy to the auditors. The NIST AI Risk Management Framework connects the aspect of trustworthiness to risk reduction and Palantir scores in all aspects.

Although the 2025 survey report of McKinsey reveals that close to two out of three organizations still have a hard time scaling AI, Palantir has been successful within such controlled settings. The company has recorded a 57% adjusted operating margin and 56% adjusted free cash flow margin in Q4. Such margins imply that deep integration is profitable.

This scale only increases during 2026 (forward guidance). The adjusted income of operations is projected to be between $4.126 billion and $4.142 billion by the management. The company also steered adjusted FCF to between $3.925 and $4.125 billion.

This really sums up why this platform creates lasting dependency. A business model has reached such an efficiency point when it is generating almost as much cash as it is operating income. This financial capability is based on the dependency that Palantir engineering builds.

The platform represents related decisions using its Ontology, as opposed to raw data points. Once the approval chains and audit trails are integrated into the software by a company, the switching cost will be a nightmare both technically and operationally.

AIP motivates growth through dependence on the platform. It shifts Palantir from a software provider into the real record of operational decisions. When a business has grown to be dependent on this automation in its day-to-day operation, the software is difficult to rip out.

This structural lock-in is a point that should be taken into consideration since it allows Palantir to earn high incremental margins. This combination is the main factor behind the growth compounding that is being projected in the 2026 perspective.

Claude vs. AIP: Why the Control Layer remains Bulletproof

The launch of Claude CoWork at the beginning of 2026 is a gigantic leap into agentic AI. Anthropic essentially transformed a chat box into a proactive digital force. It is a tool that is able to plan, browse, and execute sophisticated projects without human intervention.

Certain investors fear that such autonomous models would one day make Palantir redundant. I believe that is totally misconstrued. Claude is an excellent personal productivity tool, but it does not have the institutional oversight needed in mission-critical work.

Claude is incapable of operating a global supply chain without a strict audit trail. The record of truth and the granular permissions found in the AIP are beyond what standalone agents could possibly handle.

Practically, Palantir would consider models such as Claude to be high-performance engines but offers the chassis and steering wheel to keep them safe on the road. The layer that coordinates the models is the actual monopoly as models are commoditized.

This lock-in makes Palantir resilient even if the models become more autonomous. It is what distinguishes a smart assistant and a controlled operating system.

Palantir Secures Its Place in the Military Arsenal

Similar to how Palantir enters enterprise workflows, it is now doing so to military operations with the Maven program-of-record.

Palantir has just gotten the final Pentagon seal of approval. Last month, the Deputy Secretary of Defense officially designated the Maven Smart System as a program of record.

This is enormous since it makes Maven more than an experiment and solidly entrenched in the military arsenal with funding. The Pentagon believes that this shift will equip warfighters with the required instruments to identify and control the enemy as well as guarantee a steady stream of resources into the platform.

Consider Maven to be the main command and control brain of modern warfare. It combines huge volumes of data from satellites, drones, and radars to provide actionable intelligence on the fly.

Enemy vehicles and missile stockpiles are detected by the system at an incredibly high rate, and this greatly reduces the decision-making process on the battlefield.

Originally, in 2024 Palantir was awarded a contract worth $480 million, however, the demand soared and the Defense Department had to increase its limit to 1.3 billion by 2025. The fact that the military is counting on it for thousands of real blows is obvious.

This record status program serves as an enormous catalyst for the company. It ensures a steady flow of revenue, and Maven becomes part of the long-term military strategy. This justifies the ontology-based platform as the desirable operating system for defense workflows.

Most contractors come and go around the next budget cycle, but Palantir has just made it into the must fund list. This approval secures years of high-value contracts and provides the firm with enormous power over the future of global defense technology.

Valuation Discussion

Palantir sells at premium multiples with its forward P/E estimated at 112x for FY 26 and 80x for FY 27. These figures are projected under consensus EPS of around $1.34 and $1.89 in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

It seems like the overstretched multiples explain everything. However, to me, the market is confusing category with true value.

These estimates already include high operational momentum and increasing enterprise adoption, with revisions shifting upwards as demand picks up.

A superior lens is FCF. Palantir has generated adjusted FCF margins over 50%, which is a rare mix where profitability and growth scale together. This type of effectiveness, along with profound enterprise integration, enables the company to transform incremental revenue into disproportionate cash production.

In future, Palantir is expected to maintain 30% to 35% revenue growth per year. Such anticipation is on the back of higher adoption among current customers and consistent acquisition of new ones. It is projected that revenue will increase from about $7.2 billion in FY 26 to approximately $10.5 billion in FY 27.

The company may generate between $6 to $7 billion annually with long-term FCF margins of 40% to 45%. Using a 25x to 30x cash flow multiple would justify a valuation that compounds significantly out of the present levels.

The premium is justified and there are actual signs of the platform scaling within operations as opposed to being optional.

The stock trades around $148 (as of writing), which has support at $143 to $145. The resistance is close to $162 and $164. Notably, weekly RSI has fallen below the overbought status, and there is room for upside without immediate pressure.

The base case is pretty simple. It has Palantir pushing to $155 to 160, with consistent client growth. Bull case may go up to $170 to $180 in case there is faster adoption of enterprise AI and big deals are closed more quickly.

Even in less favorable circumstances, integration into day-to-day operations maintains a floor near $140-$145.

The market still fails to read Palantir correctly due to the tendency of the analysts to evaluate software based on the number of features and seats sold.

Palantir is able to create sustainable cash flows and retention by becoming a part of core operations, which can underpin a re-rating even if headline growth dips.

Risks To My Thesis and Takeaway

Despite the high control-layer advantage of Palantir, there are still a few risks.

First and foremost, the adoption of the enterprise can still be restricted. Unless Palantir can incorporate its platform into everyday tasks completely, revenue retention and expansion in large accounts would be slack, which would be tracked by adoption metrics.

Secondly, AI governance may turn into a commodity. The cloud providers are also developing compliance capabilities on their platforms and cross platform tools are gaining popularity. When governance becomes a standard expectation, then Palantir can experience pricing pressure despite growth in usage. It is prudent to note how the customers use the platform i.e., as a decision engine or a convenience tool.

Lastly, the dependency on government contracts makes it sensitive to changes in policy and procurement. In FY 25, the majority of revenue was in the form of public sector work, and establishing solutions at the organization level takes time and resources. Delayed deployments (an execution risk) would be a burden to margins.

Indeed, challenges are there but the history of Palantir argues that it can cope with them.

The emphasis on adoption and persistent growth helps to understand that Palantir is an essential part of how businesses function.

Being invested is real progress as the platform finds its way in the organization. This stock rewards those who appreciate the long-term benefit.
